narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid family background in the high income bracket, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges from your academic record. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 sits below the typical 3.21 average, and your LSAT score of 26 is notably lower than the dataset average of 36.70, reflecting performance in your first-year and third-year school deciles of 4 and 3—both under the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5. The model assessed your overall profile and predicted a 55% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most heavily on your LSAT score, which strongly pushed the model toward expecting failure because it was so much lower than what succeeds for most students. Next in influence was your high family income, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, likely signaling resources that help many overcome hurdles. Your undergraduate GPA followed as the third key factor, also pulling slightly toward a passing prediction despite being below average, showing it wasn't a complete drag. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your LSAT pulled you furthest from their stronger profiles, while your background advantages kept it from being a slam-dunk rejection. Full-time enrollment plans aligned with the vast majority at 92.7%, so that didn't sway things much. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive family—it's the model weighing risks based on patterns in bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that's the biggest lever here, and pair it with GPA improvement through post-bac courses or targeted prep. Strengthening these could flip future predictions in your favor at other schools. Keep pushing; many rebound from early setbacks like this.
